title: Lyapunov exponents for small random perturbations of predominantly hyperbolic two dimensional volume-preserving diffeomorphisms, including the Standard Map
speaker: Alex Blumenthal
abstract: An outstanding problem in smooth ergodic theory is the estimation from below of Lyapunov exponents for maps which exhibit hyperbolicity on a large but non- invariant subset of phase space. It is notoriously difficult to show that Lypaunov exponents actually reflect the predominant hyperbolicity in the system, due to cancellations caused by the“switching” of stable and unstable directions in those parts of phase space where hyperbolicity is violated.

proof_stages.label: Predominant hyperbolicity problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from uniform hyperbolicity and formulates the main nonuniform question: when a map is strongly hyperbolic away from a small harmful set, does the asymptotic Lyapunov exponent remain positive for typical orbits?

proof_stages.label: Deterministic cone-twisting obstruction
proof_stages.summary: For the standard map and related two-dimensional systems, the derivative is strongly expanding away from critical strips but can rotate expanding cones into contracting directions, producing a delicate deterministic cancellation problem manifested by elliptic islands and hard parameter-exclusion results.

proof_stages.label: Small iid perturbation theorem
proof_stages.summary: The deterministic problem is replaced by random compositions with small iid kicks, and the main theorem states that for all sufficiently large L, with noise exponentially small in L, the top Lyapunov exponent exists almost surely as a constant and is arbitrarily close to log L from below.

proof_stages.label: Ergodic Markov-chain base dynamics
proof_stages.summary: The random perturbations induce a Markov chain on the torus for which Lebesgue measure is the unique stationary measure, giving almost-sure constancy of the exponent and a soft Furstenberg-type positivity baseline.

proof_stages.label: Projective-bundle stationary measure formula
proof_stages.summary: The dynamics is lifted to the projective bundle of directions, and the Lyapunov exponent is bounded below by averaging one-step logarithmic derivative growth against any stationary measure for the lifted chain.

proof_stages.label: Stationarity reduction to good orbits
proof_stages.summary: Using stationarity, the stationary measure is pushed forward along a fixed n-step random itinerary, while base points are split into a small bad set that hits critical strips and a good set for which only directions ending nearly vertical can contribute negatively.

proof_stages.label: Shearing nonconcentration estimate
proof_stages.summary: Absolute continuity of the noise prevents the stationary direction measure from concentrating too much initially, and repeated good hyperbolic steps shear mass away from the vertical contracting cone; balancing this against the bad base-set volume gives the stated small-noise threshold and log L lower bound.

proof_stages.label: Computability and robustness lesson
proof_stages.summary: The proof is presented as evidence that random dynamical systems should allow parameter-specific, potentially computable Lyapunov estimates that avoid the uncheckable fine-structure exclusions of deterministic nonuniform hyperbolicity and may extend to higher-dimensional predominantly hyperbolic systems.

examples.name: Elliptic islands and local KAM tori
examples.note: Elliptic periodic points surrounded by KAM-type invariant tori illustrate why deterministic cone-twisting can defeat hyperbolicity for very long times and why the random theorem is surprising, but the proof does not route through a particular island.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explains the local KAM-torus picture around elliptic periodic points and reasons through how trajectories and tangent directions behave near such islands

examples.name: Large-noise iid matrix-product toy case
examples.note: The fully noisy regime reduces morally to products of simple iid matrices, illustrating that positivity of order log L is easy when the dynamics is overwhelmed by noise and that the real question is the small-noise threshold.
examples.engagement_reason: the large-noise iid matrix-product case is described as a graduate exercise giving Lyapunov exponent of order log L, but the speaker does not actually do the calculation

passes.reason: The large-noise limit obliterates the base dynamics into an iid matrix-product toy model, an extreme simplified benchmark.
